EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Pacific cold front with moisture surge led to periods of heavy mountain snow along the Idaho-Montana border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow fell at and around Lookout Pass in the mid-afternoon on the 21st, causing a full full blockage of Interstate 90 due to accidents and stuck vehicles. Periodic road closures continued through the morning of the 22nd with reports of 9 to 10 hours wait times for motorists attempting to cross Lookout Pass. The Lookout Ski resort reported 10 to 16 inches of snow over the 24-hour period ending on the morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low over northwest Wyoming brought wraparound moisture into southwest Montana, resulting in periods of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow of 2 to 5 inches fell across southwest Montana, but became locally heavy on Homestake Pass from the mid-evening of the 29th through the early morning of the 30th. The Basin Creek SNOTEL, located just south of Homestake Pass, reported 14 to 16 inches of snow over 12 hours. Interstate 90 was closed for about 5 hours due to the heavy snowfall rates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low over southwest Montana brought moderate to locally heavy snow the evening of March 31 into the morning of April 1. A secondary closed low over eastern Washington State enhanced the moisture transport into northwest Montana and brought around 2 feet of snow in the mountains, and snow to some of the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow of 2 to 4 inches was common. Chains were required on Homestake Pass for several hours due to lane blockages from an accident that lasted into the morning of April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low over southwest Montana brought moderate to locally heavy snow the evening of March 31 into the morning of April 1. A secondary closed low over eastern Washington State enhanced the moisture transport into northwest Montana and brought around 2 feet of snow in the mountains, and snow to some of the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From the evening of the 31st into the morning of April 1, wet snow of 1 to 4 inches caused power outages across eastern sections of the Flathead Valley as branches and trees fell onto power lines. Up to 1,600 customers were without power at one point, according to the local electric cooperative. The Noisy Basin SNOTEL, located in the Swan Range just east of the Flathead Valley, picked up 4 inches of snow water equivalent in the form of 22 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were declared across western Montana due to snow and freezing roads, resulting in moderate impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple social media reports indicated that roads became very slick during the evening of the 14th. One individual noted that while at the ER, three or four separate people were being treated for serious injuries from vehicle accidents that evening. Another person stated, 'Highway 2 between Evergreen and Columbia Falls is pure ice.' Light snow, combined with northerly winds gusting up to 24 mph at times between the GPI Airport and Columbia Falls, contributed to deteriorating conditions. Snowfall totals included 4 to 5 inches in the Kalispell area and Mission Valley, 9.5 inches at Creston, and 6 inches near Bigfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were declared across western Montana due to snow and freezing roads, resulting in moderate impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation reported severe driving conditions on Highway 200 between Potomac and Helmville, from 2100 to 2305MST on February 14. They also issued severe driving conditions on Interstate 90 between Clinton and Drummond for two hours starting at 2300MST on the 14th. Law enforcement reported several crashes and slide-offs east of Missoula on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty winds and locally heavy snow spanned two months (January and February) associated with an upper level low the dug southward from the Gulf of Alaska toward Vancouver Island. Ahead of the system a period of strong westerly to southwesterly mid level flow set the stage for a period of gusty winds across commonly windy areas over the plains (Rocky Mountain Front, MT200 corridor), in addition to the Madison River Valley and adjacent areas. Areas of snow developed between two separate waves within the digging trough, which brought a localized heavy snow report along the Southern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuation from previous month: Montana mesonet station Dog Gun Lake E (Acedoggu) measured sustained winds, averaged over an hour, of 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long cold spell for the plains was finally broken as surface troughing in lee of the Rocky Mountain Front developed Feb 19. Initially it was thought that this troughing, combined with increased zonal flow aloft would result in ground blizzard conditions along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains Feb 20-22. This did not end up panning out, as winds were not strong enough to break the crust in a meaningful way. Nonetheless, zonal flow aloft continued to increase, which eventually resulted in high winds Feb 22-24, mostly near the Rocky Mountain Front in typically windy areas. Additional strong gusts developed elsewhere, but were much more isolated over this timeframe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet sensor LGPVC Logan Pass measured a 78 mph gust. Sunpoint and the St Mary Raws also measured gusts in excess of 58 mph.

Many drivers struggled to maintain control, leading to multiple accidents with unknown injuries. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty winds and locally heavy snow spanned two months (January and February) associated with an upper level low the dug southward from the Gulf of Alaska toward Vancouver Island. Ahead of the system a period of strong westerly to southwesterly mid level flow set the stage for a period of gusty winds across commonly windy areas over the plains (Rocky Mountain Front, MT200 corridor), in addition to the Madison River Valley and adjacent areas. Areas of snow developed between two separate waves within the digging trough, which brought a localized heavy snow report along the Southern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana mesonet sensor measured sustained winds, averaged over an hour of 50 mph. This event spans two months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an increasing pressure gradient, cross barrier flow, and an upper level wave resulted in a period of falling and blowing snow, most notable from the Little Belts eastward through Fergus County, where blizzard conditions were observed on the 22nd. On the front end of the event, strong winds were observed from Cut Bank westward toward the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport measured a gust of 67 mph in addition to measuring sustained winds, averaged over an hour, of 41 mph. Montana mesonet site Mission Lake S measured a 61 mph gust.

CZ_NAME: JUDITH BASIN COUNTY AND JUDITH GAP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an increasing pressure gradient, cross barrier flow, and an upper level wave resulted in a period of falling and blowing snow, most notable from the Little Belts eastward through Fergus County, where blizzard conditions were observed on the 22nd. On the front end of the event, strong winds were observed from Cut Bank westward toward the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of public reports, AWOS observations, and law enforcement reports combine to support blizzard conditions from mid-morning through early afternoon on the 22nd. Judith Basin Sheriff reported severe driving conditions due to roads across the county being drifted over faster than they can be cleared (715 AM Post). Intermittent AWOS observations reported visibility around one-quarter mile, with gusts between 35 and 40 mph (9 AM and 11 AM). Further, a public report from social media reported visibility of 50 feet between Raynesford and Geyser from falling and blowing snow around 1 PM. These reports combine to total around or a bit more than three hours of blizzard conditions in total in this zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front brought a period of moderate to heavy snow, with lingering lighter snow in northerly upslope areas from late night Jan 23 into Jan 24. The Little Belts saw the heaviest snow, with snow totals in excess of 10 inches observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showdown ski area reported 11 inches storm total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist Pacific system interacted with an arctic air mass that gradually moved west across the Continental Divide. The higher impact area with the combination of precipitation and cold air ended up being across northwest Montana due to icy roads. The Glacier Region experienced freezing rain, snow, low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total snowfall from the storm included 8.3 inches in Kila, 6 to 7 inches in Marion, 5.5 inches in Eureka, and 4 inches in Libby. The Cabinet Mountains received 10 to 14 inches of snowfall from this event. On Friday afternoon, the 3rd, rain fell along US-2 west of Marion. After dark, black ice developed in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist Pacific system interacted with an arctic air mass that gradually moved west across the Continental Divide. The higher impact area with the combination of precipitation and cold air ended up being across northwest Montana due to icy roads. The Glacier Region experienced freezing rain, snow, low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of icy roads appeared on social media, including references to white-knuckle driving in the Mission Valley. One person mentioned that their truck began sliding, and they saw another truck in a ditch near Bigfork. Multiple slide-offs were also reported across the Flathead Valley. The icy roads developed after dark as sub-freezing air, with gusts up to 20 mph flowing out of the Glacier Region, solidified the wet roads. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches in the Flathead Valley and 6 to 8 inches in the Mission Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist Pacific system interacted with an arctic air mass that gradually moved west across the Continental Divide. The higher impact area with the combination of precipitation and cold air ended up being across northwest Montana due to icy roads. The Glacier Region experienced freezing rain, snow, low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell intermittently until the morning of February 3, when it intensified in the Bitterroot Valley. A spotter on the west side of Stevensville reported 7 inches of total snowfall over 36 hours from the event. By late afternoon, a mixture of freezing rain, ice pellets, and snow was reported and continued into the evening. After dark, reports indicated that side roads had frozen over, with snow accumulating on top. Total snowfall from the event ranged from 4 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist Pacific system interacted with an arctic air mass that gradually moved west across the Continental Divide. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter driving conditions were reported across the region. The conditions worsened at night due to re-freezing roads. The arctic intrusion was not particularly strong along and west of the Divide. At MacDonald Pass, northeast winds reached up to 14 mph, but wind chills dropped to around 12 below zero. Total snowfall was approximately 4 inches in Anaconda, while the mountains generally received 6 to 10 inches.

The Glacier Region experienced freezing rain, snow, low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads became slick by Friday evening, February 3, in the Swan Valley and along US-2 from Columbia Falls to West Glacier. The fresh snowfall of 4 to 8 inches, combined with strong winds gusting up to 35 mph, caused blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibility along the US-2 corridor���especially east of West Glacier to Marias Pass. Wind chills dropped to the negative single digits Friday night. The surrounding mountains received 0.60 to 1.00 inches of snow water equivalent which could equate to 6 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level troughing diving southeastward from the Gulf of Alaska brought a period of zonal to slightly northwesterly flow aloft to the region January 23. A strong ridgetop inversion allowed for mountain wave activity to develop, which in combination with an increased pressure gradient and cross barrier flow resulted in gusty winds along the Rocky Mountain Front out to Cut Bank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors measured winds and gusts reaching criteria, including Montana mesonet sensor Dog Gun Lake E (Acedoggu), which measured a 76 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of waves flattening an upper level ridge resulted in a strong mid level flow across the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana mesonet sensor Dog Gun Lake E (Acedoggu) measured sustained winds, averaged over an hour, of 51 mph. Additional sensors measured gusts of criteria as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist Pacific system interacted with an arctic air mass that gradually moved west across the Continental Divide. The higher impact area with the combination of precipitation and cold air ended up being across northwest Montana due to icy roads. The Glacier Region experienced freezing rain, snow, low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow intensity became heavy at Lookout Pass by the afternoon but decreased by late evening. The combination of wet roads re-freezing at sunset, falling snow, and rain likely contributed to very slick conditions and a double fatality(indirect) on Evaro Hill. A semi-truck jackknifed, rotated into the oncoming lane, and slid down US-93, crashing into a truck and killing a 19-year-old man and a 17-year-old woman. Traffic was blocked for three hours, from 5 to 8 p.m. MST, on Friday evening. Snowfall totals included: 3 to 6 inches along Highway 200 west of Thompson Falls and around 10 inches at Lookout Pass.

CZ_NAME: BEARS PAW MOUNTAINS AND SOUTHERN BLAINE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stout closed low over Manitoba swung a shortwave around its western periphery and then southward toward the Northern Rockies Feb 16. The wave stalled over MT, resulting in a strongly cyclonic flow aloft, while the main upper disturbance exited eastward.. This allowed for a long duration snow event that lasted into the 18th.||After this system cleared out, a strong surface high resulted in another period of extreme cold. This episode will also serve as the location for additional extreme cold highlights from June 16 through the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported 10 inches total over two observations. Start and criteria time were estimated.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft brought in an upper-level disturbance. This system primarily brought mountain snow and lower elevation rain, but strong westerly flow brought gusty down sloping winds over the Continental Divide. Isolated high winds were reported along the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTM73 Ledger N Mda reported a 63 mph wind gust. ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport also reported a high wind gust and high sustained winds.

CZ_NAME: JUDITH BASIN COUNTY AND JUDITH GAP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough passed through Montana and brought a few passing upper-air disturbances through the region. This brough periods of mountain snow and rain & snow at lower elevations over the span of a few days. A cold front decreased snow levels down to the lower elevations on the night of the 28th through morning of the 29th. Heavier, localized snow banding brought moderate to heavy snow in the northerly upslope areas of the Little Belts Mountains and Tobacco Roots Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 5SW of Stanford reported 8.5 inches of new snow. They also reported 7 inches of snow fell approximately over 3 hours. Station elevation was 4565 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stout closed low over Manitoba swung a shortwave around its western periphery and then southward toward the Northern Rockies Feb 16. The wave stalled over MT, resulting in a strongly cyclonic flow aloft, while the main upper disturbance exited eastward.. This allowed for a long duration snow event that lasted into the 18th.||After this system cleared out, a strong surface high resulted in another period of extreme cold. This episode will also serve as the location for additional extreme cold highlights from June 16 through the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust troughing moving inland across the Pacific NW began to usher cooler air southward on the 19th into the 20th across northern areas. In the meantime, deep mixing and weak shear with marginal instability managed to produce isolated severe gusts across SW MT on the 19th. Heading into the 20th the instability decreased, but southerly to southwesterly flow increased aloft over SW MT. This resulted in a period of strong winds and gusts, strongest in the Madison River Valley. As the Pacific troughing moved further inland, attention then turned to precipitation, which fell in the mountains as snow Saturday into Sunday (June 20-22). All the while, strong winds/gusts were occurring across portions of the Hi-Line as the cooler air surged southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station LMRM8 Antelope RAWS. Time of the gust is estimated given the station reports once per hour and reports a peak wind gust over the prior hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust troughing moving inland across the Pacific NW began to usher cooler air southward on the 19th into the 20th across northern areas. In the meantime, deep mixing and weak shear with marginal instability managed to produce isolated severe gusts across SW MT on the 19th. Heading into the 20th the instability decreased, but southerly to southwesterly flow increased aloft over SW MT. This resulted in a period of strong winds and gusts, strongest in the Madison River Valley. As the Pacific troughing moved further inland, attention then turned to precipitation, which fell in the mountains as snow Saturday into Sunday (June 20-22). All the while, strong winds/gusts were occurring across portions of the Hi-Line as the cooler air surged southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station NCRM8 Newlan Creek RAWS. Time of gust is estimated using radar given  the site reports a peak gust one time per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more convective type of rain/snow transitioned to be more stratiform across the region as several embedded waves within broad cyclonic flow consolidated across the region. Uncertainty was high with this system, closely related to how quickly snow levels fell across the region. Several areas saw snow in excess of 10 inches, including a snotel estimating 17 inches new snow in Jefferson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reports an estimated 8 inches new snow near Hardy Creek exit on I-15. Event spanned two months, hence a start time of midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow aloft continued across the Northern Rockies into August 4. An embedded wave within the troughing was favorably timed in the afternoon hours to result in scattered showers and thunderstorms. Flow aloft increased with this wave, which allowed for gusty winds to be translated to the surface. Only one instance of severe caliber winds were observed on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K1IM India measured the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although upper level ridging was in place, weak disturbances moving through the ridge resulted widely scattered showers and thunderstorms August 16 and 17. The stronger storms, which formed in deep inverted-V environments, resulted in a few instances of severe gusts, mainly across the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient weather station. Sustained winds of 47 mph at the time of observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over CA lifted northeastward toward Wyoming the 27th, which helped carve out a surface low over eastern Wyoming. This low draw a cooler Canadian airmass southward into MT. Showery precipitation transitioned to be more stratiform in nature as snow levels fell across the region. Overall, snow was heaviest in the Tobacco Roots, with lighter snow elsewhere in terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Albro Lake estimated 12 inches new snow. Criteria time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug out of British Columbia and pulled modified arctic air over the Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received so much snow that slush accumulated on the roads and caused slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported 2.1 inches of snowfall in 1.5 hours, ending at 0700MST. The Marion CoCoRaHS observer reported 3.0 inches of snow with 0.25 inches of snow-water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug out of British Columbia and pulled modified arctic air over the Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received so much snow that slush accumulated on the roads and caused slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Marias Pass, 7 inches of snow fell, accompanied by northeast winds between 7 and 12 mph, with gusts to 25 mph. Temperatures fell to 4 below zero the following morning. Java East, located at the U.S. Highway 2 bend southeast of Essex, received 3.4 inches of snow. During the morning of the 3rd, Java East picked up an additional 2.6 inches of snow between 0500 and 0600MST. Up to 10 inches fell in the mountains of Glacier National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug out of British Columbia and pulled modified arctic air over the Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received so much snow that slush accumulated on the roads and caused slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty northeast winds near 30 mph, air temperatures near freezing, and falling snow contributed to slick roads from the Flathead Valley to Flathead Lake. There were multiple reports of vehicle slide-offs along U.S. Highway 93 on the west side of Flathead Lake during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Snow fell in the Mission Valley, bringing low visibility and slushy accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug out of British Columbia and pulled modified arctic air over the Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received so much snow that slush accumulated on the roads and caused slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer located west of Anaconda received 1.2 inches of snowfall. The mountains along and west of the Continental Divide, including Georgetown Lake, received 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug south out of British Columbia, pulling modified arctic air across the Continental Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received enough snow for slush to accumulate on roads, causing slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers formed along the cold front as it slowly shifted southwards, producing strong, gusty winds over 40 mph. Power outages were reported from near Happy's Inn eastward to Marion along U.S. Highway 2. Snow was reported at Olney and Crystal Creek, but didn't accumulate on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug south out of British Columbia, pulling modified arctic air across the Continental Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Some locations received enough snow for slush to accumulate on roads, causing slick conditions during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred during this event between Essex and Marias Pass along U.S. Highway 2. Marias Pass received 7 inches of snow, accompanied by northeast winds gusting to 25 mph. The COOP observer at Essex measured 5.1 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heron COOP observer reported significant limb damage and one fallen tree. The utility company reported 259 customers without power during the early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough dug south out of British Columbia, pulling modified arctic air across the Continental Divide. This brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Georgetown Lake, MacDonald Pass, and Homestake Pass all experienced snow-covered conditions at times during the event. Strong winds up to 45 mph occurred along and west of the Continental Divide. A few power outages were reported in the Philipsburg and Butte areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient Weather Station NE of Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall and a debris flow east of Sula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bitterroot National Forest has issued a safety alert for a debris flow across Forest Road 724 near the East Fork Trailhead. The debris flow occurred within the 2024 Johnson Wildfire burn scar after a short, intense storm dropped a radar-estimated 0.41 inches of rain in just 15 minutes, and around 0.51 inches in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously high precipitable water (200-210% of normal) along with a shortwave trough, resulted in high rainfall rates with convection. The Weather Prediction Center issued a slight risk for excessive rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple debris flows blocked Skalkaho Highway (Highway 38) in three locations. Two slides occurred south of Skalkaho Falls, with an additional flow reported west of the winter gate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously high precipitable water (200-210% of normal) along with a shortwave trough, resulted in high rainfall rates with convection. The Weather Prediction Center issued a slight risk for excessive rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on the Highline Trail. People needed to be rescued, but ongoing thunderstorms prevented helicopter operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist airmass, with precipitable water values between 0.8 and 1.0+ inches, was established across the region. A passing shortwave trough provided large-scale lift, triggering widespread thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates. The overall environment supported training convection, where multiple storms repeatedly moved over the same areas. These conditions were highly favorable for producing excessive rainfall and initiating flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report described torrential rain of approximately 20 minutes in duration and  roadway ponding near Bernice���s Bakery around 1600MST; additionally, the roof of new annex started leaking.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Asos station Kgtf Great Falls Airport.

Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station GW3478 Tctiber peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded wave within more broad troughing digging into the Pacific Northwest moved across the region late night August 6 into August 7. Elevated instability increased as showers and thunderstorms moved eastward from Central Montana toward Fergus county. As these thunderstorms continued to balloon and cross Lewistown Divide, they became severe, producing a wide swath of gusts in excess of 55 mph, including a peak gust of 71 mph just east of Lewistown Divide.||Later in the day ample instability and shear developed across Fergus county again to result in an instance of large hail near Grass Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather underground station near Grass Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana mesonet station Wetzel NE peak gust from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell on the backside of a closed low, impacting higher elevations of Glacier National Park and surrounding areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the afternoon of the 21st and continued through the morning of the 22nd. Going-to-the-Sun Road was closed due to hazardous driving conditions going over Logan Pass during this period. New snowfall was estimated at 10 inches at Logan Pass, with accumulation reported as low as the Big Bend area around 4,300 feet. The top of Big Mountain Ski Area reported about 4 inches of new snow, which disrupted outdoor activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station GW6391 Gildford 10n peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of July 14th, a strong backdoor cold front moved into northwest Montana. Combined with a strong pressure gradient that was accentuated by a low-pressure system over Oregon, this front produced a period of intense and damaging downslope winds, particularly along the Mission and Swan mountain ranges. The abrupt wind shift and severe gusts created dangerous conditions across the region.||Thunderstorms with dozens of lightning strikes were also reported across northwest Montana, and many locations saw widespread wetting rains. This meteorological setup was exceptionally rare; you have to go back to the Julys of 1972, 1958, and 1951 to find a similar event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 43 mph was recorded at the Crystal Lakes Resort airport, south of Grave Creek. It was noted that winds could have been stronger in canyons, with estimates closer to 50 mph. The winds caused scattered power outages, with specific reports of an outage in the town of Troy and another outage affecting Montana Highway 56 north of Noxon.

Combined with a strong pressure gradient that was accentuated by a low-pressure system over Oregon, this front produced a period of intense and damaging downslope winds, particularly along the Mission and Swan mountain ranges. The abrupt wind shift and severe gusts created dangerous conditions across the region.||Thunderstorms with dozens of lightning strikes were also reported across northwest Montana, and many locations saw widespread wetting rains. This meteorological setup was exceptionally rare; you have to go back to the Julys of 1972, 1958, and 1951 to find a similar event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS station at Mount Aeneas (7120 ft) recorded a peak wind gust of 88 mph. Within Glacier National Park, an estimated 10 trees were downed, many of them large Lodgepole Pines with diameters up to 24 inches wide. One of these trees fell onto a parked car at the Lake McDonald Lodge, causing damage. The downed trees also contributed to an 18-hour closure of the Inside North Fork Road and the Quarter Circle Bridge Road leading to Apgar Lookout. The observer at the Thoma Lookout by the Canadian border at 7,112 feet, reported wet snow falling during the early morning hours. Numerous trees were reported on the east side of Swan Lake.

The abrupt wind shift and severe gusts created dangerous conditions across the region.||Thunderstorms with dozens of lightning strikes were also reported across northwest Montana, and many locations saw widespread wetting rains. This meteorological setup was exceptionally rare; you have to go back to the Julys of 1972, 1958, and 1951 to find a similar event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station at Kalispell (KGPI) measured a peak gust of 58 mph, with unofficial estimates of frequent gusts near 50 mph along the east shore of Flathead Lake. The winds led to extensive power outages, with over 2,500 customers without power at the peak of the event and at least eight separate instances of downed power lines reported. The winds also caused a pile burn to get away from the owner and cause a shed to catch fire, and a photo showed roof damage to a structure in Yellow Bay. Several orchards between Blue Bay and Yellow Bay sustained damage in the form of bruising and cherry, plum and leaf drop. At least three powerline-related wildfires were started: northeast of Bigfork on Highway 83 near Echo Lake, northeast shore of Whitefish Lake as a tree fell onto a line, and near Hilltop Road in Columbia Falls. Flathead Lake exhibited confused seas, with swells arriving from multiple directions. A boat broke loose in the Lakeside area. A 58-year-old male drowned after taking a rowboat out with friends Tuesday morning near Rocky Point July 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the Bitterroot Mountains and Anaconda Range, tracked north and east across western Montana and produced gusty winds of 45 to 60 mph that caused spotty damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:35 pm, the Hamilton AWOS reported southerly winds of 41 mph gusting to 49 mph. These damaging gusts caused significant disruption, leading to three separate power outages in the area. At the Hamilton Brewfest, a participant sustained a significant injury when a large tree branch broke and fell onto them. The associated storms also produced dangerous lightning, which ignited at least six new wildfires in the Bitterroot National Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the Bitterroot Mountains and Anaconda Range, tracked north and east across western Montana and produced gusty winds of 45 to 60 mph that caused spotty damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the Bitterroot Mountains and Anaconda Range, tracked north and east across western Montana and produced gusty winds of 45 to 60 mph that caused spotty damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Missoula, a wedding tent was collapsed by the winds. Northwest of the Wye, gusts were estimated to be over 50 mph. The University of Montana campus also sustained damage, with several downed trees, one of which fell onto a roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall and triggered subsequent debris flows across parts of southwest Montana. The event was exacerbated by a deep layer of atmospheric moisture, with precipitable water values measured at nearly one inch, leading to highly efficient and impactful rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar estimated 1.41 inches of rain had fallen over the western portion of the Daly burn scar, located west of Skalkaho Pass. A few days later, a rock slide was reported in the vicinity.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar-estimated rainfall of 1.77 inches fell in one and a half hours and triggered a debris flow that covered the East Fork Road, east of Sula, at Mile-marker 4.

Most of the favorable thermodynamic environment stayed off to the east of the region, with a few strong to severe storms clipping Fergus County. The primary hazard was severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTDEN Denton measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms, enhanced by anomalously high atmospheric moisture, brought periods of heavy rain and large hail to portions of west-central and southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over Butte produced hail estimated close to one inch in diameter and caused flash flooding near Schley Avenue. Over an inch of rain fell in one hour, overwhelming local storm drains and leading to a flooded basement, an overflowing manhole, and significant water ponding on streets and in nearby parking lots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms, enhanced by anomalously high atmospheric moisture, brought periods of heavy rain and large hail to portions of west-central and southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm impacted the area northwest of Missoula, producing both large hail and heavy, localized rainfall. Quarter-sized (1.00 inch) hail was estimated via photos near the Frenchtown Mill. The storm's torrential downpour resulted in an official measurement of 0.92 inches at the Missoula International Airport (KMSO), with radar estimating up to 1.40 inches nearby. This intense rainfall overwhelmed drainage systems on Broadway, causing water to flood the eastbound lane. A downed tree was reported on the northbound lane of Highway 83 near milepost 31, north of Summit Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds swept through the northern Flathead Valley, with the Elmo MDT station reporting a peak gust of 59 mph. Nearby, the Glacier Park International Airport (KGPI) recorded gusts up to 38 mph. Approximately 100 people were without power. Conditions on Flathead Lake were also hazardous, where a boater near Wildhorse Island reported high winds, white caps, and three-foot swells. Confirming the turbulent weather, a mesonet at the Flathead Lake Biological Station's Narrows site registered a peak wind speed of 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought embedded shortwaves aloft that developed thunderstorms in the region. Storms on July 1st were concentrated in Southwest MT. Coverage of thunderstorms became widespread across the region July 2nd due to better shear and moisture. The main hazards with storms were severe wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent troughing pattern brought in enough moisture and instability for isolated severe storms to form near the Hi-line in Northern Montana. One storm produced large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent troughing pattern brought in good instability and shear to produce severe thunderstorms across the region over the 3 day time frame with strong winds as the primary hazard. There was also isolated large hail. Anomalously high moisture content and slower moving storms brought localized flooding. A thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall and caused flooding in Havre and in Clear Creek on the 27th. The Havre airport reported 1.72 inches in about 45 minutes, with other reports in town estimated 2.5 of rain quickly. There were also flooding along Highway 69 & 55 in Jefferson County on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. This activity spread slowly eastward with time, moving over the plains around and after sunset. Although this activity moved in overnight as the inversion was beginning to set up, deep mixing from diurnal heating resulted in ample DCAPE that these thunderstorms were able to capitalize on. Severe gusts lasted several hours as this activity moved eastward over the plains.||Heading into the daylight hours on the 20th, the aforementioned troughing off the BC coast began its approach toward the Northern Rockies. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana mesonet station Simpson peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This episode has fire starts from mid to late August.

So far the fire has burned 963 acres of timber and brush and as of late September (83% Containment). No damage to structures were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging was initially in place across the region on the 19th, which allowed temperatures to soar into the 90s to around 100. A wave moving through the ridge ahead of troughing off the BC coast resulted in convective initiation late afternoon and early evening across western MT. Although surface temperatures were cooler than the prior day, cooling temperatures aloft more than made up for it. Winds aloft with this troughing were also sufficient for organized thunderstorms capable of both gusty winds and hail. Supercells did end up forming over the plains and lasted several hours. Most supercells avoided populated areas, so hail reports were nearly non existent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT mesonet southwest of Havre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record precipitation fell as a prolonged atmospheric river event gripped the region from December 6 through 12. Much like a garden hose being moved back and forth, the jet stream shifted the focus of this moisture plume across the Pacific Northwest, repeatedly drenching parts of northern Idaho and western Montana. Between December 10 and 11, 2025, the atmospheric river specifically focused on northwest Montana, and produced record rainfall and catastrophic rain-on-snow flooding. A saturated snowpack acted as a sponge until reaching capacity late December 10, when warm nighttime temperatures and rainfall rates of 0.30-0.50 inch per hour below 7,000 feet triggered rapid runoff. Total precipitation amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches within the 24 hours leading up to the event.||Significant flooding and landslides occurred across Lincoln, western Sanders, and western Mineral Counties, where numerous tributaries and streams  overwhelmed their banks. The event resulted in eight bridge washouts in Lincoln County, with one additional bridge lost in both Mineral and Sanders Counties. This was one of the most significant rain-on-snow December events since 1933, when 12 bridges were washed out in Lincoln County alone. On December 11, Governor Greg Gianforte issued Executive Order 9-2025 declaring a flooding disaster in northwestern Montana. Following a formal request on December 16, a Presidential Emergency Declaration was approved to provide federal aid for Lincoln and Sanders counties and the Blackfeet Nation. Preliminary assessments placed infrastructure and property damage at $35.1 million for all three counties. The only injury was from a man who drove off the Farm-to-Market Road bridge into the waters below. He was able to escape with only scrapes and bruises. This atmospheric river event also brought high winds to the mountains and a few valley locations. Precipitation levels reaching 238 percent of normal in British Columbia and the Glacier Region sent inflows into Flathead Lake soaring to over 300 percent of average. The West Central Montana Avalanche Center reported several wet loose avalanches and mudslides near steep highway cuts near Lolo Pass on December 11. They reported that the highway crew had to clear debris from the road. Rockfall was also reported along MT-1, north of Georgetown Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the mountains caused flooding and significant erosion of forest service roads in western Mineral County.   In the Superior Ranger District, the Deer Creek Bridge was compromised as the St. Regis River eroded the north bank, severely damaging the surrounding roadbed. Further west, reports from the Ward Creek drainage (National Forest Service Road 889) confirmed a complete washout, forcing the closure of a 7-mile stretch from Milepost 4.5 to 11.9.  A hunter driving out of the mountains along Big Creek reported driving through feet of water at times and encountering falling trees that blocked the road. A plugged pipe at Milepost 1.5 up the East Fork of Big Creek caused the road to be completely covered with rock and debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Notable infrastructure losses included the Farm-to-Market Road state bridge over Libby Creek, the Granite Creek county bridge, Ramsey Creek Bridge at milepost 8.9, Old Town/Libby Creek Bridge 8.1 miles up Libby Creek Road(NFS Road Number 231), West Fisher Road bridge over Lake Creek at about the 5.5 milepost, the Lower Granite Creek Bridge at milepost 2.5 on National Forest Service(NFS) Road 4791, Whoopie Creek Bridge(NFS Road 384) near Spar Lake in the Troy District, and Keeler Creek took out the Lake Creek Road county bridge(NFS Road 473) and the county levee in Troy. Additional washouts occurred on Libby Creek Road (NFS Road 231) at mileposts 0.9 and and about 25.0 and along NFS Road 384 where it was washed out due to a landslide past the Whoopie Creek Bridge. The Hiatt Creek Bridge(NFS Road 384) near Spar Lake sustained damage. While the Ross Creek bridge survived the flood, the creek bed beneath it rose significantly. Floodwaters deposited so much rock and gravel that the space under the bridge was reduced from 8 feet to 6 inches, leaving no room for water to pass. The West Fisher Road lost about 600 feet of road at milepost 25.5, and the Poorman Creek culvert washout on NFS Road 278/Bear Creek Road. The Upper Young Creek Road, NFS Road number 7202, located northwest of West Kootenai, was made impassable between milepost 3.5 and 3.6 due to a landslide. The Kootenai National Forest Service remarked that there were other bridges that incurred minor damage and they will have to repair some of the abutments. ||In the Ross Creek Cedars recreation area, the newly reconstructed facilities and trails���completed just months prior at a cost of approximately $1.9 million���sustained major damage. The access route, NFS Road 398, was compromised by multiple landslides and debris deposits, including a large slide that taken half the road and a compromised bridge where Ross Creek Road crosses Ross Creek. ||In Libby, Cherry Creek jumped its banks to flood subdivisions along Cherry Creek Drive and Claire Avenue, while Libby Creek inundated homes near Champion Haul Road and Riverdale Drive, cutting off residents and submerging properties under nearly a foot of water on Vicks Lane. The Lower Flower Creek Diversion Dam was nearly compromised by debris and high sediment, resulting in a long-term boil water advisory for the City of Libby. For a time, the 5th Street Bridge in Libby was under water. Water was also across the road on West 2nd Street Extension by Parmenter Creek. ||In Troy, Callahan Creek washed away a 15-foot rock barrier, and Iron Creek caused flooding in multiple residences. Road 427/Callahan Road had 2 washouts (3.4 mile and 6 mile). Washout on road 473/Keeler Road.  The Poorman Creek SNOTEL, located in the Cabinet Mountains south of Libby, reported 5.10 inches of rainfall in 24 hours. This marked the wettest 24-hour period within the month of December, and the third highest for any time of the year. The Yaak River near Troy crested at 8.3 feet(7,940 cubic feet per second) at 1130MST on December 11, while the Fisher River near Libby reached 7.67 feet(4,710 cubic feet per second) at 2115MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist and unstable southwesterly flow aloft across the interior west ushered in precipitable water in excess of an inch for the first of August. Flow through the column was rather slow, resulting in thunderstorms that were slow to move and became anchored to outflow boundaries. A few strong gusts were observed, with a few instances of minor flooding across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate reports of golf ball to 2 inch hail in Buffalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded wave within more broad troughing digging into the Pacific Northwest moved across the region late night August 6 into August 7. Elevated instability increased as showers and thunderstorms moved eastward from Central Montana toward Fergus county. As these thunderstorms continued to balloon and cross Lewistown Divide, they became severe, producing a wide swath of gusts in excess of 55 mph, including a peak gust of 71 mph just east of Lewistown Divide.||Later in the day ample instability and shear developed across Fergus county again to result in an instance of large hail near Grass Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K1NM Novemeber. Peak gust for the sensor.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross barrier flow developed along the Rocky Mountain Front the morning of the 26th behind a cold front that passed through the prior evening. Areas along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front out to Cut Bank were the gustiest, though given the higher criteria west of Cut Bank, only one zone had a High Wind Warning Issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low pressure system brought snow to the higher elevations of western Montana. This led to wintry impacts in Glacier National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions necessitated the closure of the Going-to-the-Sun Road within Glacier National Park. The hazardous surface was a direct result of up to 4 inches of snow that fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some northeast Montana locations experienced severe gusty winds during the afternoon hours as a low pressure system over south-central Canada was exiting the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Peck handar mesonet site (GGWFP) measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic air mass moved into northwest Montana, producing a prolonged period of impactful weather. Periods of moderate to heavy snow showers combined with strong easterly winds created hazardous backcountry conditions, particularly above 3500 feet where snow accumulation and blowing snow reduced visibility. In the higher valleys, heavy snow initially resulted in slushy, difficult travel, but impacts increased significantly by nightfall as temperatures fell below freezing. The rapid freeze led to black ice and slick roadways, especially at bridges, contributing to multiple vehicle accidents. Following the storm, an impressive low stratus layer developed and lingered through October 16 across west-central Montana, which significantly restricted Visual Flight Rules (VFR) pilot access to the area for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road report for Highway 2 between Happy's Inn and Kalispell highlighted snow and ice conditions resulting from 2 to 5 inches of total accumulation. Mountain areas received a total snow accumulation of 4 to 8 inches. Below-freezing temperatures and persistent north-to-east winds, with gusts up to 20 mph, were key factors in the rapid and widespread formation of black ice.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high-impact early-season snow and strong east wind event resulted in the closure of Going-to-the-Sun Road (GTTSR) at Logan Pass due to hazardous ice development beneath 6 to 8 inches of snow and was confirmed by plow operators. Mountain zones across the region (above 5,000 feet) received widespread snowfall ranging from 6 to 25 inches, with the highest accumulations, including 25 inches at Moss Peak, concentrated through the Swan and Mission Ranges. Several crashes and slide-offs were reported by Montana Highway Patrol. Additionally, the event was accompanied by strong winds with gusts to 50 mph along the foothills of the Swan Range, while gusts to 72 mph were recorded at Mount Aeneas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust low to mid level flow westerly flow associated with a potent shortwave and attendant cold fronts resulted in a prolonged period of high-end winds and gusts across the entire region Dec 17 into the 18th. Multiple gusts in excess of 90 mph were observed in populated areas, with a peak observed gust of 104 mph west of Bynum. Prolonged power outages were observed across the region, mostly due to trees being damaged/falling over completely. Additional societal impacts included extremely difficult to impossible travel conditions, which prompted a Civil Emergency Message for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front the afternoon of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Dec. 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to the region, creating a disaster of a magnitude rarely seen in our December climate record across all counties of western Montana. Saturated soils combined with recent record warm temperatures weakened root structures and topsoil, substantially exacerbating treefall. Governor Gianforte issued an executive order declaring a wind disaster, and multiple counties enacted local emergency declarations. The American Red Cross opened an emergency shelter in Superior in Mineral County, and the Butte Civic Center supported residents without power. While impactful December winds have occurred previously���such as the Dec. 4, 1995 Lolo National Forest blowdown that felled over 7 million board feet of timber and a Dec. 15, 2006 event with 65 mph gusts���this storm significantly eclipsed those precedents, delivering frequent gusts between 60 and 90 mph in valleys and over 100 mph in the mountains for multiple hours, particularly between 0800MST and 1200MST Dec. 17. Sandwiched between two other lesser wind events, this system caused widespread damage, blocking roads, hiking, and cross-country ski trails with widespread treefall, and crushing vehicles and structures���including a home near Polson where a resident narrowly escaped with bruises as a tree destroyed their home. Utility infrastructure sustained severe damage; Flathead Electric Cooperative described it as one of the most challenging events in their 88-year history. This extent of damage forced utilities to request outside mutual aid last week to assist with repairs. Power outages persisted for over a week in some locations. The event prompted preemptive school closures based on National Weather Service Missoula messaging and emergency travel alerts. Extended outages led to significant household and grocery store food loss. Northwest Montana, previously impacted by historical flooding, was impeded by wind damage to communications infrastructure which hampered emergency services.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0940 MST, the Lincoln County Sheriff���s Office issued an Emergency Travel Only order as downed trees and power lines rendered major routes���including US-2 and MT-56���impassable. In Libby, a failure of the cellular network created a communications gap for emergency services during the peak of the event.||Property damage was widespread; numerous homes and structures were struck by falling timber, including a home 4 miles NW of Troy and a garage up Pipe Creek. Private losses were notable, including multiple totaled greenhouses and several vehicles crushed by falling Ponderosa pines, such as a late-model truck near the Libby airport and another at Bull Lake. Utility infrastructure was hit hard; numerous downed poles resulted in a total system outage for Lincoln Electric, while Northern Lights and Flathead Electric reported extensive damage to lines, with some Libby-area customers remaining without power for nearly a week. Blacktail Mountain Ski Area was forced to suspend lift operations due to storm-related complications with lift lines.|Due to mild temperatures and additional precipitation, the Yaak River nearly reached minor flood stage on December 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just two days after a historic windstorm devastated the region, another strong atmospheric river-connected wind event impacted the area during the early morning hours of December 19, severely compounding ongoing recovery efforts. Heavy snow combined with strong winds brought blizzard conditions in the backcountry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds brought blizzard conditions to the backcountry, severely impacting mountain passes on December 18 and 19. SNOTEL sensors recorded massive gains, including a 17-inch snow depth increase at Lolo Pass and 12 inches at Saddle Mountain. |Travel conditions deteriorated rapidly on the afternoon of December 18 as intense snowfall rates���including 1 inch per hour near West Fork���led to rapid accumulation on roadways despite ongoing mitigation efforts. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph at Lost Trail Pass created localized blizzard-like conditions with near-zero visibility. The treacherous weather caused severe transportation disruptions on US-12, triggering a chain requirement between mile markers 0 and 4 by 1913MST. The following morning, an incident completely blocked both lanes at milepost 2.0, leading to an official declaration of Severe Driving Conditions that lasted through midday on December 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just two days after a historic windstorm devastated the region, another strong atmospheric river-connected wind event impacted the area during the early morning hours of December 19, severely compounding ongoing recovery efforts. Heavy snow combined with strong winds brought blizzard conditions in the backcountry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As conditions rapidly deteriorated on the afternoon of December 18, highway officials issued a chains required restriction at 1530MST. Just fifteen minutes later, at 1545MST, a semi-truck spun out in the hazardous conditions, completely blocking the westbound lanes of the highway. The Lookout Pass SNOTEL reported a 5-inch snow depth increase.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Dec. 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to the region, creating a disaster of a magnitude rarely seen in our December climate record across all counties of western Montana. Saturated soils combined with recent record warm temperatures weakened root structures and topsoil, substantially exacerbating treefall. Governor Gianforte issued an executive order declaring a wind disaster, and multiple counties enacted local emergency declarations. The American Red Cross opened an emergency shelter in Superior in Mineral County, and the Butte Civic Center supported residents without power. While impactful December winds have occurred previously���such as the Dec. 4, 1995 Lolo National Forest blowdown that felled over 7 million board feet of timber and a Dec. 15, 2006 event with 65 mph gusts���this storm significantly eclipsed those precedents, delivering frequent gusts between 60 and 90 mph in valleys and over 100 mph in the mountains for multiple hours, particularly between 0800MST and 1200MST Dec. 17. Sandwiched between two other lesser wind events, this system caused widespread damage, blocking roads, hiking, and cross-country ski trails with widespread treefall, and crushing vehicles and structures���including a home near Polson where a resident narrowly escaped with bruises as a tree destroyed their home. Utility infrastructure sustained severe damage; Flathead Electric Cooperative described it as one of the most challenging events in their 88-year history. This extent of damage forced utilities to request outside mutual aid last week to assist with repairs. Power outages persisted for over a week in some locations. The event prompted preemptive school closures based on National Weather Service Missoula messaging and emergency travel alerts. Extended outages led to significant household and grocery store food loss. Northwest Montana, previously impacted by historical flooding, was impeded by wind damage to communications infrastructure which hampered emergency services.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A historic high wind event impacted the Flathead and Mission Valleys on December 17, 2025, as a powerful cold front brought high winds acting on soils super-saturated by a preceding atmospheric river. Peak gusts frequently exceeded 60 mph across the valley floors, including 67 mph at 0855MST at Glacier Park International Airport, 64 mph at 0915MST at Elmo, and 62 mph at 0900MST at the Creston Agrimet station. The Flathead County Sheriff's Office issued a travel advisory at 1000MST due to hazardous conditions and widespread disruptions. Significant transportation impacts included the closure of US-93 near Somers after a semi-truck was blown across the roadway, and multiple lane blockages on MT-35, MT-200, and Big Mountain Road between 0740MST and 1313MST due to fallen trees and power lines.||Utility infrastructure sustained damage that Flathead Electric Cooperative described as unprecedented and one of the most challenging in their 88-year history. At the 1200MST peak, over 30,000 meters were without power. The combination of high winds and unstable root structures sent trees into lines, causing over 150 individual outages. In Lake County, 5,166 Mission Valley Power customers remained without power as late as 1500MST, with some outages persisting past 1800 MST. These extended outages forced many grocery stores and residents to dispose of all fresh and frozen inventory after being without power for over eight hours.||Property damage was widespread; falling timber caused roof damage to homes in Polson and significant structural damage at the historic Conrad Mansion in Kalispell, where a tree destroyed a gazebo. Public and recreational icons were also affected; the iconic Bigfork cross was destroyed. Flathead County officials described the aftermath as devastating, and final power restoration for the last Flathead Electric members was not completed until 1000MST on December 24.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind reports across the Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys included 88 mph at the Stevensville Airport at 0956MST, 68 mph at the Little Rock Creek RAWS at 1009MST, 66 mph at the Stevi RAWS at 1014MST, and 61 mph at the Missoula Airport at 0853MST. Hazardous conditions and flying debris severely impacted travel. The Missoula Police Department advised against all unnecessary travel at 0933MST. At 0915MST, fallen trees blocked the left lane of S-203 Southbound, and at 1026MST, US-93 was completely blocked by fallen trees between mile markers 4 and 5. In Missoula, a massive tree fell across West Broadway at Birch Street, pinning a driver inside a vehicle, though they were not injured. Downed trees and power lines also forced the sustained closure of Gharrett Street around 1345MST. In Ravalli County, the Sheriff's Office issued an Emergency Travel Only order as power outages disabled traffic lights and debris blocked numerous roads, including Daly Avenue and Old Corvallis Road. ||Falling timber caused catastrophic damage to utility infrastructure. By 1500MST, approximately 30 percent of Ravalli County (7,433 customers) and nearly 7,000 customers in Missoula County were without power. A prominent transmission pole was left leaning precariously over North Reserve Street near Costco in Missoula. Critical services were interrupted; the Ravalli County Airport lost power around 0914MST, and the Hamilton VA Clinic was forced to close completely due to a lack of power and water. Outages were so extensive that they persisted for thousands of customers well into December 19, and final power restoration for Missoula Electric Cooperative members was not completed until 0300MST on December 24. ||Structural and property damage was severe. Around 0930MST, intense winds ripped sections of the roof off Jefferson School in Missoula. In Frenchtown, a community pavilion near the elementary school was destroyed. Numerous massive trees crashed into homes and vehicles, including a large pine striking a house between Stevensville and Florence, massive fir trees landing on homes on South Third and Fourth Streets in Hamilton, and a spruce tree crushing two trucks on Pine Street in Stevensville. Schools across the region, including in Frenchtown and Hamilton, were forced to close or issue shelter-in-place holds as conditions deteriorated and phone lines failed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust low to mid level flow westerly flow associated with a potent shortwave and attendant cold fronts resulted in a prolonged period of high-end winds and gusts across the entire region Dec 17 into the 18th. Multiple gusts in excess of 90 mph were observed in populated areas, with a peak observed gust of 104 mph west of Bynum. Prolonged power outages were observed across the region, mostly due to trees being damaged/falling over completely. Additional societal impacts included extremely difficult to impossible travel conditions, which prompted a Civil Emergency Message for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front the afternoon of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few sensors in this zone measured gusts of 58 mph or greater or sustaiend winds of 40 mph or greater averaged over at least an hour, including RAWS stations EKHM8 Elkhorn and GAAM8 Galena, and mesonet stations MTSPK Spokane Creek (MT DOT) and MTCLN Clancy (MT DOT). Public reports were also received from Montana City and Clancyof pine trees down blocking roadways. THe peak observed gust was at Spokane Creek (75 mph).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme winds battered the higher elevations, with a weather station atop Mount Sentinel (5,026 feet) serving as a proxy for the surrounding peaks.  This station recorded a sustained wind of 76 mph and a staggering peak gust of 99 mph from the west-northwest at 0900MST, with gusts consistently exceeding 70 to 90 mph throughout the morning and into the afternoon. Along Highway 12 between Lolo and Lolo Pass, the Montana Department of Transportation warned motorists to limit travel as strong winds blew trees, debris, and rocks onto the roadway around 0820MST west of milepost 32.6. Widespread power outages were reported throughout the area, including significant disruptions up Petty Creek noted by emergency services on the evening of December 17.||Recreational areas experienced staggering timber blowdowns, with snowmachine and cross-country trails covered by hundreds of fallen trees. The Bitterroot Cross-Country Ski Club described it as one of the biggest windstorms they had experienced in years. At the Lolo Pass Visitor Center, officials noted extensive damage with trees completely blocking all cross-country trails. At Chief Joseph Pass, the Bitterroot Cross-Country Ski Club reported hundreds of downed trees; crews had to clear over 150 trees on December 18 just to reopen three main skiable trails. The massive cleanup efforts extended for months; the Missoula Snowgoers Snowmobile Association reported removing 62 trees from a single trail near Lolo Hot Springs as late as February 17, 2026. In the Bitterroot National Forest, Lost Horse Road had multiple trees down across the road and sustained damage to campgrounds.

The one DOT sensor at Lewistown Divide lost power and did not report winds/gusts for the overwhelming majority of the event. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Dec. 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to the region, creating a disaster of a magnitude rarely seen in our December climate record across all counties of western Montana. Saturated soils combined with recent record warm temperatures weakened root structures and topsoil, substantially exacerbating treefall. Governor Gianforte issued an executive order declaring a wind disaster, and multiple counties enacted local emergency declarations. The American Red Cross opened an emergency shelter in Superior in Mineral County, and the Butte Civic Center supported residents without power. While impactful December winds have occurred previously���such as the Dec. 4, 1995 Lolo National Forest blowdown that felled over 7 million board feet of timber and a Dec. 15, 2006 event with 65 mph gusts���this storm significantly eclipsed those precedents, delivering frequent gusts between 60 and 90 mph in valleys and over 100 mph in the mountains for multiple hours, particularly between 0800MST and 1200MST Dec. 17. Sandwiched between two other lesser wind events, this system caused widespread damage, blocking roads, hiking, and cross-country ski trails with widespread treefall, and crushing vehicles and structures���including a home near Polson where a resident narrowly escaped with bruises as a tree destroyed their home. Utility infrastructure sustained severe damage; Flathead Electric Cooperative described it as one of the most challenging events in their 88-year history. This extent of damage forced utilities to request outside mutual aid last week to assist with repairs. Power outages persisted for over a week in some locations. The event prompted preemptive school closures based on National Weather Service Missoula messaging and emergency travel alerts. Extended outages led to significant household and grocery store food loss. Northwest Montana, previously impacted by historical flooding, was impeded by wind damage to communications infrastructure which hampered emergency services.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme wind gusts battered the region, including 76 mph near Avon at 1017MST, 62 mph at the Yankee Flat RAWS at 1040MST, and 58 mph at the Bearmouth MDT at 1132MST. MacDonald Pass on US-12 experienced prolonged severe conditions following a cold front passage at 0847MST, which delivered an initial peak gust of 62 mph, then a maximum sustained wind of 51 mph recorded at 1430MST and gusts peaked at 75 mph at 1700MST. Bert Mooney Airport recorded a peak gust of 67 mph at 1353MST. ||Butte-Silver Bow Emergency Management issued a severe weather alert at 0925MST, urging residents to remain indoors, limit travel to emergencies only, and avoid windows due to flying debris, downed trees, and live power lines. By 1330MST, reports indicated high winds left trees either sheared mid-trunk or completely toppled throughout the Butte area. The top half of a massive fir tree snapped on Quartz Street in Uptown Butte, and a spruce tree planted in the 1930s crushed and totaled a parked car in the same neighborhood. The storm compromised the power grid with live downed lines triggering several small ground fires. The destruction was so severe that a representative from the Montana Standard described it as the worst wind damage they had ever witnessed. Law enforcement maintained an emergency-only travel advisory as crews worked throughout the day to clear streets of fallen trees and debris. Also a downed power substation was reported in the Helmville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific cold front traversed the Northern Rockies on Friday, December 26, 2025, bringing convective snow showers and gusty winds. Behind this initial front, an Arctic boundary began spilling over the Continental Divide into western Montana on Saturday morning, December 27. The steepening low-level lapse rates and intense frontal forcing created an environment primed for organized, convective snow squalls. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme gusts battered Sanders and Mineral Counties on December 17, with peak wind reports including 69 mph at 0800MST at the Plains AWOS and 57 mph at the Thompson Falls RAWS. The dangerous conditions prompted an urgent Do Not Travel alert from the Community Ambulance Service of Western Sanders County at 0830MST because live power lines and falling trees completely blocked roadways. Major transportation corridors were heavily impacted: a downed tree blocked a lane of I-90 Westbound at 0725MST, fallen trees blocked the Eastbound I-90 Tarkio exit at 0845MST, a semi-truck crash completely blocked MT-200 at 0940MST, and downed power lines forced the closure of Highway 10 West near Huson at 1120MST.||Utility infrastructure suffered catastrophic damage from falling timber. In Mineral County, over 80 percent of tracked customers (4,002 meters) lost power. Sanders County experienced similar devastation, with 4,481 customers, roughly 47 percent of the county, losing electricity. The power outages were so widespread and prolonged that approximately 250 residents utilized a warming shelter in Mineral County two days after the initial event. High-elevation recreation was also impacted, with Lookout Pass Ski Resort losing power by 0735MST, alongside reports of widespread timber blowdowns across its ski runs.||Structural and property damage was severe and widespread across the zone. At 0930MST, severe winds ripped sections of the roof off Thompson Falls Elementary School. In Haugan, falling timber struck the Savenac Historic Tree Nursery and Visitor Center, causing significant roof damage. In Huson, an uprooted tree lifted a camper and twisted it entirely off its jacks. Meanwhile, at Quinn's Hot Springs south of Paradise, an estimated 75 large trees were toppled by the winds, compounding the massive cleanup efforts required across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific cold front traversed the Northern Rockies on Friday, December 26, 2025, bringing convective snow showers and gusty winds. Behind this initial front, an Arctic boundary began spilling over the Continental Divide into western Montana on Saturday morning, December 27. The steepening low-level lapse rates and intense frontal forcing created an environment primed for organized, convective snow squalls. These squalls produced localized areas of heavy snow, rapid accumulations, and near-zero visibility, leading to dangerous flash freeze conditions and hazardous travel on regional highways, including Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall impacted Interstate 90 from east Missoula to Bearmouth. This prompted the activation of a Wireless Emergency Alert (WEA) for communities including Clinton, Potomac, and Greenough due to continued whiteout visibilities and dangerous, life-threatening travel conditions. Earlier on the 27th, strong winds associated with the frontal passage brought trees down north of Seeley Lake, blocking Highway 83 near mile marker 26.
