EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just two days after a historic windstorm devastated the region, another strong atmospheric river-connected wind event impacted the area during the early morning hours of December 19, severely compounding ongoing recovery efforts. Heavy snow combined with strong winds brought blizzard conditions in the backcountry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hungry Horse RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 66 mph between 0311MST and 0411MST, followed by subsequent gusts of 55 mph and 57 mph through 0611MST.||The high winds caused immediate and severe utility disruptions. Flathead Electric Cooperative reported that the main lines feeding West Glacier and the Canyon were down. By 0730MST, approximately 8,600 members across their service territory had lost power. While crews made significant progress throughout the day, the Flathead County Sheriff's Office reported that approximately 3,000 electric meters remained without power by 1600MST.||Additional property and infrastructure damage was reported within Glacier National Park and the surrounding corridors. Park dispatch reported two 12-inch diameter trees fell at the foot of Lake McDonald, and another tree blocked a local bike path. In the Apgar area, a large fir tree crashed onto a cabin, requiring a crane for removal. Travel was also impacted, with the Montana Department of Transportation reporting 8 to 9 downed trees blocking the route between Essex and Goat Lick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river pushed into the Northern Rockies while a dry and very cold air mass was firmly entrenched across western Montana and north-central Idaho. This pre-existing cold pool proved highly resilient, and high temperatures largely remained at or below freezing in the valleys. As the warmer atmospheric river moisture eventually overran the sub-freezing surface layer, the snow transitioned into a dangerous period of freezing rain across several valleys. Because heavy cloud cover during the day prevented the sun from sufficiently heating the pavement, road surface temperatures remained stubbornly below freezing. This resulted in rapid black ice formation that caused severe travel disruptions, accidents, interstate highway closure, and at least one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were generally light across the zone, ranging from 1 to 3 inches in the Libby and Yaak areas. Roadways including Highway 2, Highway 37, and Highway 56 became covered in snow, slush, and ice, leading to slick travel during the morning commute.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTGYS Geyser, AWOS station K1CM Charlie, and MT mesonet stations Judith Gap, Moccasin, and Benchland all measured gusts of 58 mph or greater. The peak gust was at Geyser, and was 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river pushed into the Northern Rockies while a dry and very cold air mass was firmly entrenched across western Montana and north-central Idaho. This pre-existing cold pool proved highly resilient, and high temperatures largely remained at or below freezing in the valleys. As the warmer atmospheric river moisture eventually overran the sub-freezing surface layer, the snow transitioned into a dangerous period of freezing rain across several valleys. Because heavy cloud cover during the day prevented the sun from sufficiently heating the pavement, road surface temperatures remained stubbornly below freezing. This resulted in rapid black ice formation that caused severe travel disruptions, accidents, interstate highway closure, and at least one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers reported of 10 inches of snow at Lost Trail Pass. Meanwhile, possible freezing rain was reported along US-12 west of Lolo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slick conditions contributed to several accidents across southwest Montana due to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along Interstate 90 between Drummond and Garrison Junction, freezing rain contributed to at least 6 accidents. At 1125MST a small car lost control, slid into the eastbound lane and crashed into a tractor trailer. All three people in the car were declared dead(indirect) on the scene. The road conditions were described as wet and icy.

CZ_NAME: BEARS PAW MOUNTAINS AND SOUTHERN BLAINE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust low to mid level flow westerly flow associated with a potent shortwave and attendant cold fronts resulted in a prolonged period of high-end winds and gusts across the entire region Dec 17 into the 18th. Multiple gusts in excess of 90 mph were observed in populated areas, with a peak observed gust of 104 mph west of Bynum. Prolonged power outages were observed across the region, mostly due to trees being damaged/falling over completely. Additional societal impacts included extremely difficult to impossible travel conditions, which prompted a Civil Emergency Message for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front the afternoon of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous sensors in this zone measured sustained winds of 40 mph or greater averaged over at least an hour, or gusts of 58 mph or greater, including RAWS station FBLM8 Fort Belknap and LBCM8 Little Bullwhacker Creek, Montana mesonet stations Dry Lake, Cleveland N, Warrick S, and Big Sandy SE. An additional public report was received stating gusts similar to what these sensors observed. The peak observed gust in this zone was observed on the Fort Belknap reservation at the Fort Belknap RAWS (88 mph) between 618 PM and 718 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall. While the event began with heavy snow across the region, a transition to rain and freezing rain occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While valley locations saw 2 to 3 inches of snow before transitioning to rain overnight, sub-freezing surface temperatures created hazardous pockets of freezing rain. The Cabinet Mountains bore the brunt of the storm, recording over two inches of snow water equivalent, equating to approximately 2 feet of new snow; however, because the moisture plume was so narrow, accumulations decreased sharply to the north, with mountains near the Canadian border receiving only 6 to 10 inches. Snow-covered roads were observed during the onset of the snow, particularly at Yaak Hill, Bull Lake, and Crystal Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level troughing propelled a Canadian cold front through the region, leaving a northerly to northwesterly upslope flow in its wake. This upslope enhancement led to isolated instances of heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 6.5 inches storm total snow 5 miles west of Hilger. Start, criteria, and end time are estimated.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak brought an initial round of mainly mountain snow across the region December 4th into early December 5th. After a break, a more potent wave progressed across the Northern Rockies, bringing additional heavy snow. All in all, mountains seeing the heaviest snow were across the Central Montana ranges and southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple SNOTEL sites estimated snowfall of 9 inches or greater, including Beaver Creek (9 inches), Black Bear (10 inches), Clover Meadow (9 inches), Shower Falls (13 inches), and Lone Mountain (9 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial Canadian cold front brought a much colder airmass southward through North-central Montana to nearly the I-90 corridor on the 11th. As a mid level warm front overran this airmass, instances of heavy snow were observed from the 12th into the 13th. Cold air near the surface and warm nose aloft between the Helena area and Townsend area resulted in significant icing the morning of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter 5 NNE Loma reports 7 inches new snow in total by the report time. Snow was heavy at the time of the report but we never received a follow-up report with a final total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering arctic air was overrun by a moist plume of subtropical moisture on December 1 and 2. The northwest flow pattern brought snow to both valley and mountain locations. Although the air temperature drop was gradual, road temperatures were above freezing initially. This caused the initial snowfall to melt and then re-freeze, creating a layer of ice beneath the snow. These slick road surfaces led to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations varied across northwest Montana, with 1 to 2 inches reported in Yaak and heavier amounts between 3 to 5 inches from Libby east to Marion. The Montana Department of Transportation reported severe driving conditions on U.S. Highway 93 from Eureka to Dicky Lake and on Highway 37 west towards Lake Koocanusa.

These slick road surfaces led to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in the valleys ranged from 5 and 6 inches between Hungry Horse Dam and West Glacier, with up to 7 inches at Essex. The mountains received 8 to 12 inches of new snow. Along U.S. Highway 2 between Devil���s Creek and Marias Pass, wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow, thereby reducing visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering arctic air was overrun by a moist plume of subtropical moisture on December 1 and 2. The northwest flow pattern brought snow to both valley and mountain locations. Although the air temperature drop was gradual, road temperatures were above freezing initially. This caused the initial snowfall to melt and then re-freeze, creating a layer of ice beneath the snow. These slick road surfaces led to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 5 inches created icy and hazardous conditions across the region. At Lookout Pass, a crash was reported at 1518MST, with webcams showing vehicle slide-offs by 1630MST. Chains were required at the pass by 1650MST. Later that evening, a crash blocked both eastbound lanes of Interstate 90 east of Alberton starting at 2100MST, where road conditions showed ice. Near the Idaho border, a crash blocked both lanes of Montana Highway 200 between mileposts 0 and 2 near Heron at 2253MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering arctic air was overrun by a moist plume of subtropical moisture on December 1 and 2. The northwest flow pattern brought snow to both valley and mountain locations. Although the air temperature drop was gradual, road temperatures were above freezing initially. This caused the initial snowfall to melt and then re-freeze, creating a layer of ice beneath the snow. These slick road surfaces led to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although snowfall totals were modest, ranging from 1.0 to 1.7 inches in Missoula and less than an inch east of Florence, the societal impact was high due to a rapid transition from wet to icy roads. Early afternoon sunshine had warmed pavement temperatures above freezing, causing the initial snowfall at 1620MST to melt on contact. This created a layer of slush and water that was quickly packed down by heavy rush-hour traffic. As the sun set and temperatures continued to fall, this compacted slush froze into a solid, bonded sheet of ice.||The Missoula City Police Department issued a MEANS alert at 1915MST for emergency travel only due to hazardously icy conditions. By 2016MST, the Missoula County Sheriff's Office alerted drivers to avoid Upper Miller Creek Road as it became impassable. Conditions gradually improved as road crews treated the ice, and emergency travel alerts were lifted by 2314MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall. While the event began with heavy snow across the region, a transition to rain and freezing rain occurred in some valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall. While the event began with heavy snow across the region, a transition to rain and freezing rain occurred in some valleys.

While valley accumulations included 7.4 inches in Clinton, 7.5 inches in Seeley Lake, and 9.4 inches at Lindbergh Lake, the surrounding mountains were buried under 2 to 3 feet of new snow. The immense weight of this heavy, wet snow resulted in power outages across the Seeley Lake area during the morning and afternoon of December 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall. While the event began with heavy snow across the region, a transition to rain and freezing rain occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley snowfall amounts varied, with 4.5 inches reported in Drummond, 2 inches in Butte, and a notable 8.2 inches measured by an observer located 7 miles west of Anaconda. Higher elevations saw even greater totals, including 7 inches at Nevada Ridge, 8 inches at Copper Camp, and up to 10 inches across the Anaconda Range. Severe driving conditions were declared east of Butte including Homestake Pass at 2331MST December 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering arctic air was overrun by a moist plume of subtropical moisture on December 1 and 2. The northwest flow pattern brought snow to both valley and mountain locations. Although the air temperature drop was gradual, road temperatures were above freezing initially. This caused the initial snowfall to melt and then re-freeze, creating a layer of ice beneath the snow. These slick road surfaces led to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the Potomac and Seeley Lake regions were light, generally ranging from 0.8 to 2.5 inches. While road temperatures at Bonner reached the mid-30s during the afternoon, northwest winds cooled surfaces toward the freezing mark before snow began. Road temperature sensors at Greenough Hill and Bearmouth remained near or below freezing through the afternoon. Because pavement temperatures had stabilized closer to the air temperature, the widespread melt-refreeze cycle seen in lower valleys was avoided. Impacts were limited to scattered slick spots and standard snow-covered surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved into the Marias Pass area between 2200 MST Thanksgiving evening to midnight, and caused temperatures to plummet into the teens with gusty northeast winds. As an upper level trough dug southeastwards towards southern Idaho, the Arctic air began to move more on Friday morning the 28th. Flash freezing of the roads impacted areas east of Missoula and also north to the Mission Valley, which contributed to several motor vehicle accidents. Strong frontogenesis occurred east of Missoula through the Highway 200 corridor, where over 1 inch per hour snowfall rates occurred. The Arctic air began to move down Evaro Hill towards the Missoula airport after 0800 MST Friday morning. Thursday night was mild which kept temperatures up, and also the antecedent warm ground conditions, thanks to a record warm November, probably contributed to the snow melting. Below zero temperatures were reported Saturday morning along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation reported multiple accidents, several of them with injuries. Severe driving conditions from Elliston to MacDonald Pass along US Highway 12.  Observers reported four to six inches of snow.  Winds gusted to 33 mph at Helmville.  Visibility at Bert Mooney Airport (KBTM) in Butte was below one-half mile from 1235 to 1320 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance brought a winter system to the region. This brought moderate mountain snow with localized heavy snow at the mountain peaks. A strong Pacific cold front created snow squalls along the I-90 corridor. There were isolated thunderstorms ahead of the snow squalls, which brought localized severe wind gusts at Bozeman. The cold front also produced localized high winds in Southwest MT. Along the Hi-line, a warm front brought freezing rain during the morning.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This serves as a placeholder for ongoing wildfires that started in a previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork fire started on 8/19 23 miles north of West Yellowstone in the Custer Gallatin National Forest. The fire has been ongoing throughout the whole month of September. As of 9/1, the fire burned 963 acres of timber (grass and litter understory) and brush, and was 80% contained. As of September 31st, the fire is still at 963 acres at a 90% containment. No damages were reported with the wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough with an embedded disturbance aloft produced isolated showers and thunderstorms across the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough produced afternoon showers and thunderstorms. A weak CAPE profile kept the severe thunderstorm risk on the low end. However, with the upper-level low overhead with embedded mini vorticity maximums, a brief landspout was able to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via media: Funnel cloud was observed picking up dust for about 45 seconds and believed to have touched the ground. Location was about 6 mi south of Buffalo and 2 mi west of Garneill. No damages were reported with the landspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 17, 2025, the entire region experienced a long-duration, high-impact wind event resulting in widespread damage and impacts. While strong winds are a normal occurrence across this region, this event stands out due to its spatial extent and prolonged period of impact, with many locations experiencing high sustained winds and wind gusts for 6 or more hours. Damage reported as a result of this weather system includes numerous, downed trees and power lines. Widespread severe driving conditions were reported with multiple road closures as a result of blown-over semis and trailers and fallen debris. Numerous weather observation stations across the reservation recorded wind gusts over 50mph for several hours. Strong winds lingered into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated due to 93 mph just east of the zone in the Crazy Mountains, and 82 mph gust just south of the zone at klvm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally high moisture amounts and considerable instability, along with a weak disturbance, induced a couple of rounds of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms marched northward across west-central Montana with gusty outflow winds. A gust of 59 mph occurred between Avon and Finn along Highway 141. Similar gusts occurred across the Windy Rock Fire southwest of Finn. No damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought clear skies and dry air, and this allowed overnight temperatures to drop near freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures dropped to 28-32 degrees at Libby, Marion, and similar-elevation locations for the first time in the fall season, leading to significant frost and damage to area gardens. Frosty mornings continued through the rest of the week.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning low temperatures dropped to 28 degrees at typical cold spots such as Polebridge and Condon for the first time in the fall season leading to significant frost and damage to area gardens. Frosty mornings continued the rest of the week.
